game_name: Fire Emblem: Shadow Dragon and the Blade of Light

danger_signals.visual_form: Distinguished enemy sprites with unique portraits and positioning
danger_signals.meaning: High-priority threats that must be defeated to win the map; represent critical enemies

danger_signals.visual_form: Major antagonist sprites with thematic dark/draconic visual design
danger_signals.meaning: Ultimate threats to the world; visual manifestation of the central conflict

danger_signals.visual_form: Unit removal from roster after defeat; visual loss from the army on map
danger_signals.meaning: High-consequence combat; any unit loss is permanent and affects story progression

danger_signals.visual_form: Palette-swapped unit sprites indicating different military allegiances
danger_signals.meaning: Identifies which faction controls a given area or chapter

safety_signals.visual_form: Unique character portraits with distinctive appearance; friendly sprite palette
safety_signals.meaning: Potential allies and party members; safe to engage with for recruitment

safety_signals.visual_form: Specific map tile graphics distinct from open terrain
safety_signals.meaning: Safe locations for resource acquisition (healing items, weapons, currency from scripted/player events)

safety_signals.signal: Princess Nyna and the Fire Emblem
safety_signals.visual_form: Legendary artifact and royal character presence
safety_signals.meaning: Symbol of hope and legitimacy; player forces represent the rightful kingdom

safety_signals.visual_form: Central character sprite with unique portrait; game ends only if Marth falls
safety_signals.meaning: Represents player agency and the core narrative thread; his survival is mandatory

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Falchion (the sacred sword) and Fire Emblem (shield artifact)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Gra betrays Altea; Gra sprites would show enemy palette if encountered
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Altea castle; player base of operations

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Shadow Dragon imagery; association with dark sorcery
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Medeus as primary boss sprite; generic Dolhr troops in enemy palette
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dominated territories across Archanea; primary invasion force

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Allied with Dolhr in conquest; enemy troops from Macedon appear in chapters
Allied with Dolhr; enemy palette indicates Grust forces
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Controls territories that must be liberated
Territory under magical conquest; source of dark magic
Allied territory; provides reinforcements and support
Marth's refuge; safe haven providing initial army
Kingdom of Archanea; represents rightful rule and resistance against Dolhr

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Pre-game: Era of peace under restored Archanea (100 years before events)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Historical narrative context; establishes the world as previously stable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Kingdom relationships are formal and structured; trade and diplomacy possible

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Shadow Dragon Medeus revives and allies with Gharnef and kingdoms of Macedon and Grust
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy faction presence increases; hostile sprites appear across multiple territories
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Altea betrayed by Gra (visual shift from ally to enemy); Cornelius defeated; mass invasion

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Marth in exile on Talys (refuge period)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Island nation isolated from main continent; safe zone visually distinct
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Pirate invasions as localized threats; Marth demonstrates leadership through repelling them

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Marth begins liberation campaign; rescues Aurelis
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Previously enemy-controlled territory transitions to allied control; sprite palette shifts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Recruitment of Duke Hardin and retainers; army grows; alliance formalizes

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Meeting with Princess Nyna and receiving the Fire Emblem
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fire Emblem artifact acquisition; legendary item appears in player inventory/story context
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Legitimacy conferred on Marth; resistance movement officially rallies behind him

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Liberation of Archanea from Grust occupation
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Archanean territory visually shifts from occupied (enemy palette) to liberated (allied palette)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Grust forces withdraw or are defeated; population behavior shifts from oppressed to hopeful

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Final confrontation with Medeus and Gharnef
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss sprites appear in climactic map; Shadow Dragon and dark wizard as distinct visual entities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Remaining enemy forces make final stand; ultimate threat requires Falchion and Fire Emblem to defeat

environmental_storytelling.example: Enemy-controlled vs. allied-controlled territories shown through map tile ownership and NPC behavior; visual distinction indicates political control

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character class and equipment as worldbuilding
environmental_storytelling.example: Mounted units (knights, pegasus riders) indicate mobility and military sophistication of a faction; mage units signal connection to magical states like Khadein

environmental_storytelling.technique: Map progression through linear chapter sequence
environmental_storytelling.example: Story advances through sequential map unlocks; each chapter's geography and faction composition communicates the campaign's progress across Archanea
Towns exist as resource hubs within contested territories; their presence signals inhabited regions and supply lines critical to army logistics
Commander and boss units are visually separated or elevated; their placement on the map communicates strategic importance and role in faction leadership
Unit removal from roster is visually permanent; affects roster composition in subsequent chapters, showing cumulative cost of the campaign

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves as player-controlled unit; initiates recruitment conversations; must survive all chapters
Appears as friendly unit; joins Marth's army early; represents personal alliance
Appears as boss unit in final chapter; represents the core threat to the world
Boss sprite; wields dark magic; allied with Medeus; appears as critical enemy unit
Palette-swapped sprites indicate allegiance; move and attack according to faction strategy; can be defeated and removed from map
Appear as neutral or allied units; respond to Marth's recruitment attempts; join player roster upon recruitment dialogue
Provides military support and reinforcements; represents formal alliance with rescued kingdom
Appears to confer Fire Emblem artifact; gives Marth formal backing; represents rightful rule

notes: Fire Emblem: Shadow Dragon and the Blade of Light uses discrete visual systems to communicate faction identity, world-state changes, and political alignment. The paradigm relies on sprite palettes, character portraits, and map tile control to indicate ownership and allegiance. Permanent death mechanics reinforce the high-stakes nature of the conflict visually and mechanically. Unused graphics (palette swaps, angled arrows, unused class sprites) suggest the developers iterated on visual communication systems. The game's linear progression through chapters tied to geographical liberation creates a cumulative narrative of reclamation, with each map transition representing geopolitical shifts. Class assignments and equipment serve dual purposes: mechanical function and worldbuilding (mounted units as mobility, mages as magical regions). Boss units are visually distinguished and positioned as narrative focal points, making enemy commanders immediately recognizable.
